{
  "report": "Vulnerability Path:
lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/CpsTransformer.java:905-905: prepostfixOperatorSuffix(...) : String
lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/CpsTransformer.java:905-905: ... + ... : String
lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/CpsTransformer.java:431-431: methodName : String
lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/CpsTransformer.java:432-432: methodName

Explaination: The source is a method that constructs a string based on an operation type, which could potentially be influenced by external input if the operation type is externally controlled. The sink is a method invocation using reflection (MethodCallExpression), which dynamically calls a method based on the string constructed at the source. If the methodName is controlled by an attacker, it could lead to execution of arbitrary methods, potentially leading to OS command injection if those methods interact with the system environment or execute commands.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report is a false positive. The alleged sink in `CpsTransformer.makeNode` is not a command-execution sink and not Java reflection in the CWE-078 sense; it only constructs a Groovy AST `MethodCallExpression` for the internal CPS `Builder`. The supposed source is also mischaracterized on this path: `visitPrefixExpression`/`visitPostfixExpression` derive `methodName` only from the parsed operator token, and `prepostfixOperatorSuffix` can return only `Inc` or `Dec` for `++`/`--` and otherwise throws. So the only possible method names are `prefixInc`, `prefixDec`, `postfixInc`, and `postfixDec` on a statically typed `Builder` object. Those methods merely create `ExcrementOperatorBlock` nodes implementing Groovy `++/--` semantics, not OS command execution. In real Jenkins/workflow use, this code just rewrites Groovy AST into CPS form; it does not give an attacker a path to inject shell commands or execute arbitrary system processes. The core error is incorrect source/sink selection (B1), with additional loss of type/value constraints in the path.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "1) The code explicitly says it is building AST, not executing methods: `CpsTransformer` comments state that it 'produce[s] another Groovy AST tree that invokes Builder' and 'we aren't calling Builder directly here' (lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/CpsTransformer.java:82-93). The reported sink is `parent.call(new MethodCallExpression(BUILDER, methodName, makeChildren(body)))` (CpsTransformer.java:431-432), which only creates a `MethodCallExpression` node. 2) The receiver is a fixed internal type: `BUILDER_TYPE = ClassHelper.makeCached(Builder.class)` and `BUILDER = new VariableExpression(\"b\", BUILDER_TYPE)` (CpsTransformer.java:1421-1434). 3) The method name is constrained to four constants: `visitPrefixExpression` uses `\"prefix\" + prepostfixOperatorSuffix(exp.getOperation())` and `visitPostfixExpression` uses `\"postfix\" + prepostfixOperatorSuffix(exp.getOperation())` (CpsTransformer.java:904-917); `prepostfixOperatorSuffix` returns only `\"Inc\"` for `PLUS_PLUS`, `\"Dec\"` for `MINUS_MINUS`, else throws (CpsTransformer.java:925-933). 4) The only reachable Builder methods are benign helpers: `prefixInc`, `prefixDec`, `postfixInc`, and `postfixDec` in Builder.java:550-571 each return `new ExcrementOperatorBlock(...)`; they do not invoke `Runtime.exec`, `ProcessBuilder`, or any OS command API. 5) `ExcrementOperatorBlock` just models `++/--` by calling `next` or `previous` on the operand value (`operatorMethod` is `next`/`previous`; see lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/impl/ExcrementOperatorBlock.java:23-25, 73-76). That is ordinary Groovy operator semantics, not a shell-command path.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "report": "Vulnerability Path:
plugin/src/main/java/org/jenkinsci/plugins/workflow/cps/replay/ReplayAction.java:227-227: getSubmittedForm(...) : JSONObject
plugin/src/main/java/org/jenkinsci/plugins/workflow/cps/replay/ReplayAction.java:236-236: form : JSONObject
plugin/src/main/java/org/jenkinsci/plugins/workflow/cps/replay/ReplayAction.java:236-236: getString(...) : String
plugin/src/main/java/org/jenkinsci/plugins/workflow/cps/replay/ReplayAction.java:267-267: replacementMainScript : String
plugin/src/main/java/org/jenkinsci/plugins/workflow/cps/replay/ReplayAction.java:268-268: replacementMainScript : String
plugin/src/main/java/org/jenkinsci/plugins/workflow/cps/replay/ReplayAction.java:280-280: replacementMainScript : String
plugin/src/main/java/org/jenkinsci/plugins/workflow/cps/replay/ReplayAction.java:291-291: replacementMainScript

Explaination: The source is a user-submitted form, which is a common entry point for tainted data. The sink involves using this potentially tainted data in a script execution context without any evident sanitization, which can lead to OS Command Injection if the script execution is not properly sandboxed or restricted.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported dataflow is real, but it is not a CWE-078 command-injection flaw. `doRun` accepts edited Pipeline source from the replay form and schedules a new Pipeline run with that source. That is the intended purpose of the replay feature. The source is therefore an authorized script-editing input, not ordinary untrusted data being interpolated into an OS command. The path is reachable only through replay permissions, and for unsandboxed Pipelines it is further restricted to administrators and approved scripts. So this is not an A1/A2 reachability mistake; the main error is misclassifying the sink.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "In `ReplayAction.doRun` the form value `form.getString(\"mainScript\")` is passed to `run(...)` only after `isEnabled()` and `isReplayableSandboxTest()` succeed (`ReplayAction.java:222-236`). `isEnabled()` requires `run.hasPermission(REPLAY)` and, once execution is loaded, allows replay of unsandboxed builds only for `Jenkins.ADMINISTER` (`ReplayAction.java:155-173`). `isReplayableSandboxTest()` repeats that unsandboxed-admin check (`ReplayAction.java:184-195`). In `run2`, unsandboxed replays must pass `ScriptApproval.get().using(...)` or the replay is rejected (`ReplayAction.java:287-294`). Replay also preserves the original sandbox mode by constructing `new CpsFlowExecution(script, sandbox, ...)` with `execution.isSandbox()` (`ReplayAction.java:297-298`, `ReplayFlowFactoryAction.java:66-75`), and sandboxed execution is wrapped in `SandboxContinuable` (`CpsThread.java:122`). The tests explicitly confirm the intended model: non-admin users cannot replay unsandboxed builds, while users with `REPLAY` may replay sandboxed builds (`ReplayActionTest.java:239-277`). Finally, the reported path does not call an OS-command API such as `Runtime.exec` or `ProcessBuilder`; the sink is Pipeline Groovy replay, not direct shell execution.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report is a false positive for CWE-078. The reported code in `SerializableScript.readObject` only deserializes a `Map` and copies its entries into Groovy binding variables. That is state restoration, not OS command construction or execution. No command-execution sink such as `Runtime.exec`, `ProcessBuilder`, shell invocation, or equivalent appears on the reported path. The surrounding code shows this data comes from the plugin's own persisted pipeline checkpoint (`program.dat`) and is reloaded during resume, so this is internal persistence logic rather than a real command-injection path. If there were any security concern here, it would be about unsafe deserialization/trust of persisted state (CWE-502), not CWE-078. The report explanation itself even states that the issue is 'not specifically OS Command Injection', which confirms the mismatch with the target CWE.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "`lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/SerializableScript.java:29-31` contains only `Map m = (Map) ois.readObject();` followed by `getBinding().getVariables().putAll(m);`. `putAll` into a Groovy `Binding` is not an OS command sink. The real persistence flow is internal checkpointing: `plugin/src/main/java/org/jenkinsci/plugins/workflow/cps/CpsThreadGroup.java:638-639` writes state with `RiverWriter ... w.writeObject(this);`, `plugin/src/main/java/org/jenkinsci/plugins/workflow/cps/CpsFlowExecution.java:1037-1039` stores it as `new File(owner.getRootDir(), \"program.dat\")`, and `plugin/src/main/java/org/jenkinsci/plugins/workflow/cps/CpsFlowExecution.java:929-937` reloads it with `new RiverReader(programDataFile, ...)` and `CpsThreadGroup g = (CpsThreadGroup) u.readObject();`. This shows the object stream is used for Jenkins pipeline resume state, not for building or executing OS commands. In real deployment, exploiting this path would require tampering with Jenkins controller build-state files; the reported `putAll` operation itself still would not constitute CWE-078.",
  "new_reason:": "The report also reflects C1 prompt/CWE misalignment because its own explanation reclassifies the issue as unsafe deserialization rather than OS command injection."
}",
  "report": "Vulnerability Path:
lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/SerializableScript.java:30-30: readObject(...) : Object
lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/SerializableScript.java:30-30: (...)... : Map
lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/SerializableScript.java:31-31: m

Explaination: The source involves deserialization of an object from an ObjectInputStream, which can be a vector for various attacks if the input is not trusted. The sink involves adding all deserialized data directly to script variables without any sanitization, which could lead to execution of arbitrary code if the deserialized objects are crafted maliciously. This forms a potential vulnerability path for code execution, not specifically OS Command Injection, but related to unsafe deserialization (CWE-502)."
}
4:
{
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The source is real: `doRun` reads `form.getString(\"mainScript\")` and passes it into `run`/`run2`, so the reported dataflow is reachable. However, the reported sink is not a CWE-078 sink. `ScriptApproval.get().using(replacementMainScript, GroovyLanguage.get())` is part of Jenkins script-approval bookkeeping/checking for Groovy Pipeline scripts; it does not invoke an OS shell or execute commands. For unsandboxed scripts it acts as a guard and throws if the script is not approved. Real execution happens later through Jenkins Pipeline replay (`CpsFlowExecution`), which is the intended feature and is protected by permission checks: replay requires `REPLAY`, and replay of unsandboxed builds additionally requires `Jenkins.ADMINISTER`. Therefore this report is a false positive. The main error is incorrect sink selection (B1), with a secondary conceptual conflation of privileged Pipeline script replay with OS command injection.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "Relevant code path:\n1. User-controlled input enters at `ReplayAction.doRun`: `if (run(form.getString(\"mainScript\"), replacementLoadedScripts) == null) { ... }`.\n2. In `run2`, the flagged sink is only an approval check:\n`if (!execution.isSandbox()) {`\n`    ScriptApproval.get().configuring(replacementMainScript, GroovyLanguage.get(), ApprovalContext.create(), true);`\n`    try {`\n`        ScriptApproval.get().using(replacementMainScript, GroovyLanguage.get());`\n`    } catch (UnapprovedUsageException e) {`\n`        throw new Failure(\"The script is not approved.\");`\n`    }`\n`}`\nThis rejects unapproved unsandboxed scripts; it is not OS-command execution.\n3. The replayed script is then passed into the normal Pipeline engine:\n`actions.add(new ReplayFlowFactoryAction(replacementMainScript, replacementLoadedScripts, execution.isSandbox()));`\nand `ReplayFlowFactoryAction.create(...)` does `return new CpsFlowExecution(script, sandbox, owner, hint);`.\n4. Permission model blocks unprivileged abuse:\n`isEnabled()` requires `run.hasPermission(REPLAY)` and only allows unsandboxed replay when `exec.isSandbox() || Jenkins.get().hasPermission(Jenkins.ADMINISTER)`.\n`isReplayableSandboxTest()` again requires `Jenkins.ADMINISTER` for non-sandboxed executions.\n`REPLAY` is explicitly defined as a permission: `new Permission(..., \"Replay\", ..., Item.CONFIGURE, PermissionScope.RUN)`.\n5. Tests confirm this is intended and guarded behavior:\n`ReplayActionTest.rebuildNeedScriptApproval` verifies an unapproved unsandboxed replay fails with `The script is not approved.` and no new build starts.\n`ReplayActionTest.permissions` verifies non-admin users cannot replay unsandboxed builds, while sandboxed replay is intentionally allowed for users with `REPLAY`.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}",

Explaination: The source 'getString("mainScript")' retrieves a string from a form, which can be user-controlled and thus tainted. The sink 'ScriptApproval.get().using(replacementMainScript, GroovyLanguage.get())' uses this string in a context that could potentially execute code, especially if the script is not properly sandboxed or approved. This flow from a tainted source to a sensitive execution point could lead to OS Command Injection if the script contains malicious commands."

{
  "report": "Vulnerability Path:
lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/impl/BlockScopeEnv.java:52-52: getLocalVariable(...) : Object
lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/impl/LocalVariableBlock.java:39-39: getLocalVariable(...) : Object
lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/impl/ContinuationPtr.java:69-69: o : Object
lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/impl/ContinuationPtr.java:71-71: o

Explaination: The source 'getLocalVariable' retrieves a local variable which could be influenced by external input, making it a potential taint source. The sink involves reflection to invoke a method with the object 'o' as an argument, which could lead to executing arbitrary methods if 'o' is controlled by an attacker. This setup could potentially lead to OS command injection if the method invoked is related to command execution and the input is not sanitized.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report does not describe a real CWE-078 path. `BlockScopeEnv.getLocalVariable` and `LocalVariableBlock.get` only fetch a value from the CPS interpreter environment and pass it to the next continuation. The claimed sink, `ContinuationPtr.receive -> m.invoke(target, o)`, is not an OS-command-execution sink: the reflected `Method` is resolved in advance from hard-coded internal class/method pairs, and `o` is only the data argument to that already-fixed callback. So even if the local variable were attacker-controlled, this path only advances the interpreter state; it does not let an attacker choose a method to invoke or execute a shell command. The FP is primarily due to incorrect sink selection (B1), with some type/structure blindness because the analysis ignored that `target` is an internal `ContinuationGroup` object and `methodName` is constant.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "`BlockScopeEnv.getLocalVariable` just returns a stored local or delegates to the parent env (`lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/impl/BlockScopeEnv.java:50-52`). `LocalVariableBlock.get` simply forwards that value via `k.receive(...)` (`.../LocalVariableBlock.java:38-40`). In `ContinuationPtr`, the reflective method is fixed at construction time: `resolveMethod(type, methodName)` -> `m = type.getMethod(methodName, Object.class)` (`.../ContinuationPtr.java:32-38`), and `receive` only does `m.invoke(target, o)` (`.../ContinuationPtr.java:69-71`). The `target` is bound from internal continuation objects via `ptr.bind(this)` in `ContinuationGroup.then` (`.../ContinuationGroup.java:38-40`). All discovered `ContinuationPtr` creations are static final constants with literal method names on internal `ContinuationImpl` classes, e.g. `fixLhs`, `fixName`, `fixArg` in `FunctionCallBlock` (`.../FunctionCallBlock.java:139-141`), whose bodies merely store/interprete values (`.../FunctionCallBlock.java:74-87`). Real external command execution in Jenkins Pipeline is performed by steps like `sh`/`bat`, and the README explicitly states those steps are defined in other plugins, not here (`README.md:27-35`). For untrusted Groovy calls, this project also routes calls through `SandboxInvoker` / `Checker.checkedCall` (`lib/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/groovy/cps/sandbox/SandboxInvoker.java:15-18`).",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}
